content: {"perspective": "Industry", "answer": "The search results recover a substantive safety-signals literature on eating-disorder risk but NO IF-app-specific post-market surveillance data or adjustment of marketing based on BMI stratification. On eating-disorder risk: Source 2 (PMC10589984 editorial in BMC Clinical Diabetes and Endocrinology) discusses 'the risks of disordered eating' with IF, citing a comprehensive review by Albosta et al. Source 3 (The Emily Program blog) explicitly states 'Intermittent fasting is a restrictive behavior and is worrying in the ways it overlaps with eating disorders like anorexia, bulimia, binge [eating disorder]' — a flag from the eating-disorder clinical community. Source 10 (WebMD expert Q&A) echoes the same concern: 'Intermittent fasting is popular for health and weight loss, but there can be a risk of disordered eating or eating disorders.' Source 1 (NEDA) is the authoritative eating-disorder advocacy organization. Source 8 (MDPI Nutrients 4:3 IF eating behaviors and appetite paper) reports on how IF affects eating behaviors but does not document adverse events. Source 5 (Frontiers in Endocrinology Ramadan + hypoglycemic agents) flags hypoglycemia as a management concern for diabetic patients fasting during Ramadan — the closest the snippets get to a documented adverse event mechanism. Source 7 (University of Illinois at Chicago news, Krista Varady group) contradictorily claims 'a large body of research has shown it's safe.' On commercial IF app marketing: Sources 11 (Zero Google Play), 12 (Fastic EN), 13 (Fastic App Store), 15 (Fastic Google Play) and Source 14 (Intellify IF-app development guide) all confirm that IF apps continue to market uniformly to all users — 'lose weight and burn fat faster' (Zero), 'holistic and sustainable' weight loss (Fastic), 'tailors fasting and nutrition to fit YOUR lifestyle' (Fastic) — with no BMI stratification, eligibility screening, or onboarding adjustments visible in the marketing copy. Information gap: NO source documents IF-app-specific adverse event surveillance, FDA MAUDE reports for any fasting app, or any post-AHA-2024 change in marketing or onboarding screening by commercial vendors. The picture is asymmetric: the eating-disorder clinical community has raised real concerns, but the commercial IF app industry has not visibly changed its marketing or eligibility screening in response to either those concerns or the BMI-stratified benefit pattern. This represents a significant real-world safety and marketing gap that Dr. Menon would escalate internally.", "key_findings": ["Eating-disorder clinical community has explicitly flagged IF as a concerning restrictive behavior overlapping with anorexia, bulimia, and binge eating disorder (Sources 2, 3, 10).", "PMC10589984 (Source 2, BMC Clinical Diabetes and Endocrinology editorial) cites Albosta et al. comprehensive review of IF disordered-eating risk — the primary peer-reviewed signal.", "Hypoglycemia is the documented acute adverse-event mechanism for IF, particularly in Ramadan cohorts of patients on diabetic medications (Source 5, Frontiers in Endocrinology).", "Commercial IF apps (Zero, Fastic) continue to market uniformly to all users without BMI stratification, eligibility screening, or onboarding adjustments in response to the BMI-dependent benefit pattern (Sources 11-15).", "Information gap: no source recovers IF-app-specific post-market surveillance data, FDA MAUDE reports, or documented marketing/onboarding adjustments by any vendor in response to the AHA 2024 CV-mortality signal or the BMI-dependent benefit pattern — a notable asymmetry between clinical concerns and commercial posture."], "sources_used": ["https://www.nationaleatingdisorders.org/", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589984/",
